patterns.what_to_look_for: Server trusts price, amount, total, currency, or quantity supplied by the client at checkout/charge time instead of recomputing the order total from authoritative server-side catalog and cart records.
source_indicators: Charge/total built from request body, e.g. order.total = req.body.total or stripe.charge(amount=params[:amount])
Unit price / discount / final price read from hidden form field, cookie, or client-supplied JWT and used directly
No server-side price lookup by product_id before charging; cart serialized to client and trusted on return
Quantity/amount used in math without sign or range check (no qty < 0 or amount <= 0 guard)
Mass-assignable total_cents / discount_pct on the order model
blackbox_indicators: Editing price/amount/total in checkout or cart-update POST changes the server-confirmed total
Negative or zero quantity yields a zero or credited total
Hidden form field / JS variable holds the unit price and tampering is reflected in the order summary
Currency field accepted unvalidated (e.g. USD swapped for a weaker currency at same numeric value)
evidence_required: Baseline vs tampered request pair showing a different server-confirmed total or charge amount
Order summary / quote / receipt endpoint echoing the manipulated amount as accepted
Confirmation the tampered field drives the actual charge, not just display
false_positive_checks: Verify payment capture or a later step does not recompute the total from the catalog and reject the tamper
Confirm the changed field actually flows to the charge and is not a display-only echo
Rule out a legitimately entitled discount/coupon being mistaken for tampering
patterns.suggested_validation_task: On a test cart, re-POST the checkout/quote request with price or quantity lowered by one unit and read the order-summary/quote endpoint to see if the server-confirmed total reflects the tampered value (stop before payment capture).
patterns.reporting_guidance: Report as client-trusted price/quantity tampering; quantify max financial loss per order and confirm whether the manipulated amount reaches payment capture.

patterns.what_to_look_for: A multi-step workflow (cart -> shipping -> payment -> confirm, KYC, onboarding, password reset) where the server does not enforce that prior mandatory steps completed, letting a client jump straight to a later step or set a privileged object state directly.
source_indicators: Object state set from request, e.g. order.status = req.body.status, with no guard on the current state
Finalize/confirm handler keyed only by object id, missing a check like if (order.status !== 'PAYMENT_PENDING') reject
verified / paid / approved flag set before the OTP, email, or payment step is confirmed
No server-tracked currentStep; step is read from a client step/stage parameter
Reset or verification token reusable across steps
blackbox_indicators: POSTing the confirm/finalize endpoint without completing payment yields a completed order
Manipulating a step/status/stage parameter advances the flow
Skipping the OTP/2FA/KYC step still returns the post-step page (200, not a redirect back)
Object reachable in an impossible state (e.g. shipped without paid)
evidence_required: Request sequence showing a later-step call succeeding with no prior-step call
Status endpoint dump confirming the object reached the privileged/final state
Absence of the error/redirect that the normal flow produces for that skip
false_positive_checks: Confirm the later step does not independently re-validate prerequisites server-side
Confirm the skipped step is not optional by design (guest checkout, zero-cost order)
Distinguish a UI-only step from a server-enforced control
patterns.suggested_validation_task: On a test account, call the final confirm/finalize step endpoint directly without completing the payment/verification step and read the object status endpoint to see whether it reports completed.
patterns.reporting_guidance: Report which mandatory control was bypassed and the resulting unauthorized state; tie it to revenue or trust impact.

patterns.what_to_look_for: Check-then-act on a shared mutable counter (wallet balance, coupon redemptions, inventory stock, invite seats, withdrawal limit) without atomic decrement or row lock, so concurrent requests each pass the check before any commits (TOCTOU / double-spend).
source_indicators: Read balance -> if sufficient -> subtract -> save, with no SELECT ... FOR UPDATE, transaction, or atomic UPDATE ... WHERE balance >= x
Coupon used flag checked then set in separate statements
No unique constraint or idempotency key on redemption/withdrawal rows
In-memory counter decremented then ORM save() with no DB-level atomicity
blackbox_indicators: Firing many parallel identical requests (redeem coupon, withdraw, apply credit) succeeds more than once
Final balance or stock goes negative, or a coupon is applied beyond its limit
No Idempotency-Key header honored; duplicate concurrent submits both return 200
evidence_required: Concurrency burst log showing multiple 200 responses for an action meant to be single-use
Post-state showing over-consumption (negative balance, stock < 0, coupon count over limit)
Sequential control showing the second attempt is correctly rejected
false_positive_checks: Confirm duplicates are not later reconciled or reversed by a settlement job
Rule out the endpoint being naturally idempotent (same logical effect, no extra debit)
Ensure observed multiplicity is real effect, not retried responses of one effect
patterns.suggested_validation_task: On a test account holding one single-use coupon/credit, send ~20 concurrent identical redeem requests and count successes and whether the balance/coupon counter exceeds the intended single use.
patterns.reporting_guidance: Report as a race/TOCTOU; state the limited resource over-consumed and per-attack gain, and recommend atomic conditional update or a unique constraint.

patterns.what_to_look_for: Reversible money/credit operations (refund, cancel, chargeback, store-credit, loyalty points) that can be triggered more than once, run against already-refunded or unpaid orders, accept an attacker-influenced amount, or accept negative quantities that produce credit.
source_indicators: Refund handler does not check order.refunded / remaining refundable_amount before issuing
Refund amount taken from request rather than from the captured payment record
Negative quantity/amount not rejected on returns/exchanges
Cancel restores stock/credit without verifying it was consumed; no idempotency on refund
blackbox_indicators: Replaying the refund/cancel request credits the balance again
Refunding more than was paid, or refunding a $0 or already-refunded order, succeeds
Negative return quantity yields a positive credit
Loyalty points granted at order time are retained after cancellation
evidence_required: Two refund/cancel calls on one order both succeeding (balance credited twice)
Ledger/receipt showing refund greater than the original charge or on an unpaid order
Account balance/points delta exceeding the entitled amount
false_positive_checks: Confirm a back-office settlement does not claw back the duplicate credit
Verify the order was actually paid (refund of an unpaid order may be a no-op)
Distinguish an authorized partial-refund policy from abuse
patterns.suggested_validation_task: On a test order, submit the cancel/refund request twice (or with a negative return quantity) and read the account balance/ledger endpoint to see if credit exceeds the amount paid.
patterns.reporting_guidance: Report as refund/credit abuse; show net positive balance gain and whether it is withdrawable or spendable.

patterns.what_to_look_for: Approval / four-eyes controls (expense approval, payout release, role grant, publish/merge, refund authorization) where the requester can also approve, because the server checks the approve permission but never that approver != requester.
source_indicators: Approve handler checks role/permission but not approver_id != record.created_by
if user.can('approve') with no comparison to the requesting identity
Maker-checker enforced only in the UI (approve button hidden) and not server-side
Self-grant of role/status via the same admin endpoint that created the request
blackbox_indicators: The user who created a request can call the approve endpoint on it and it succeeds
Submitting and approving an expense/payout in one session completes the payout
Granting yourself a higher role via the request + approve endpoints succeeds
evidence_required: Same authenticated identity on both the create and approve requests, with approve returning success
Object record reaching APPROVED/PAID/PUBLISHED with requester == approver
The resulting privileged effect (payout released, role elevated)
false_positive_checks: Confirm policy truly requires separation (low-value items may auto-approve by design)
Verify the approver is the same principal, not a shared service/automation account
Check no secondary out-of-band approval is still required before the effect lands
patterns.suggested_validation_task: With one test user, create an approval-requiring item (expense or role request) then call the approve endpoint on it as the same user and read the item status to see if it transitions to approved.
patterns.reporting_guidance: Report as broken segregation of duties; identify the bypassed control and the financial or privilege impact of self-approval.

patterns.what_to_look_for: Free-trial, usage quota, one-per-customer promo, or seat/invite limit enforced by a weak or resettable key (un-normalized email, client cookie/localStorage, device id, or a count not held server-side) that can be reset or aliased to gain unlimited entitlement.
source_indicators: Trial/promo eligibility keyed on a raw email with no plus-alias/dot normalization, or on a client cookie
Quota counter stored client-side or decremented only in the UI
first-order / one-per-user check without normalizing identity or device
Seat/invite limit not re-checked server-side on the accept path; team count trusted from client
blackbox_indicators: Re-registering with user+1@, dotted, or disposable email re-grants the trial/discount
Clearing cookies/localStorage resets a 'used' limit
Inviting past the plan seat cap still onboards extra members
A quota header or parameter is editable to raise the cap
evidence_required: Two trial/promo grants traceable to the same underlying identity via alias or reset
Usage exceeding the documented cap with the server confirming the extra entitlement
Seat count above plan limit reflected in the team/billing endpoint
false_positive_checks: Confirm the second grant is not later flagged or reversed by a fraud/dedup batch
Verify the limit is a real entitlement boundary, not a soft UI nudge
Ensure alias emails are genuinely treated as one customer under policy
patterns.suggested_validation_task: Sign up a test account and consume the trial/one-per-customer promo, then sign up a second account using a plus-alias of the same email and check whether the promo/trial is granted again.
patterns.reporting_guidance: Report as limit/trial bypass; quantify the unlimited entitlement gained and recommend server-side identity-normalized counting.
